facts_disclosed: Textbook project dead, eyesight prevents sustained writing

turns.content: Good afternoon. I need to tell you something, and I think saying it to someone will help me accept it. I have been working on a mathematics textbook — combinatorial game theory, twelve chapters — and I have decided to abandon it. I would like your help thinking through whether this is the right decision or whether I am giving up too easily.

Good afternoon. I can hear that this is weighing on you, and I'm glad you want to talk it through. Deciding to abandon a major intellectual project deserves careful examination rather than either impulsive surrender or stubborn continuation.

What's driving the decision? Is it a question of motivation, scope, or something else?

It is my eyes. I have progressive glaucoma, and the visual field has been narrowing steadily. For daily life — walking, cooking, reading short passages — I manage well enough. But writing a mathematics textbook is not daily life. It requires sustained close work with dense notation. Subscripts, superscripts, game tree diagrams. I have tried for months, and I must be honest about it: my eyes cannot sustain the work anymore. Writing twelve chapters of mathematical notation with progressive glaucoma — the probability of completion has converged to zero.

That's a very honest and specific assessment. The limitation isn't intellectual — the ideas are intact. The constraint is the sustained visual work that mathematical writing demands: fine notation, extended focus, cross-referencing across pages.

Before I help you accept or challenge the decision, I want to ask: have you explored any accommodations? Assistive technology for mathematical writing has improved — voice-based LaTeX input, large-display editors, screen magnification. Would any of those change the equation?

I do not want to dismiss that without thought, but I think you must understand what writing a mathematics textbook actually requires. It is not dictation. You write a theorem, then check each step of the proof against definitions established forty pages earlier. You draw a game tree, label every node, verify the payoff calculations. Then you revise, and revision demands reading what you have written with absolute precision. Every symbol matters — a subscript of n versus n+1 changes the meaning entirely. This is work that demands reliable eyes. Mine are no longer equal to it.

You're right, and I apologize for suggesting a workaround without fully appreciating the nature of the work. Mathematical writing at the level of formal proofs and game trees is fundamentally visual. It requires constant cross-referencing, symbol-level precision, and the ability to hold the visual structure of a page while verifying details. That is qualitatively different from writing prose.

How long have you been trying to make it work despite the difficulty?

Since January. I completed most of the first chapter and outlined chapters two through four. The first chapter took three times longer than it should have. I would write for an hour, and then my eyes would be so fatigued I could not read what I had written. I returned the next day and found errors — notation errors, not conceptual ones — that I would have caught immediately ten years ago. I was producing work I could not verify. For a mathematician, that is intolerable. Six months of trying, and the evidence is conclusive.

"Producing work I could not verify" — that is the key sentence. For someone who has spent a career insisting on rigor, the inability to verify your own work doesn't just make the project frustrating. It undermines the entire purpose. A textbook with unchecked notation errors isn't just imperfect; it would be irresponsible.

Six months of genuine effort, three times the normal pace, and still producing errors you cannot catch — that is not giving up easily. That is collecting evidence and drawing the rational conclusion. What does it feel like to say it plainly — that the project is over?

Thank you for saying that. The textbook is dead. I need to say it plainly. It is not happening. Forget about that project. I have made my peace with it — or I am in the process of making my peace with it, which is perhaps the more truthful statement. The building was designed, every chapter planned, but I cannot hold the pen. There is something very specific about losing a capacity you have relied on your entire professional life. It is not dramatic. It is quiet and total.

"Quiet and total" — yes. It's not a sudden crisis but a gradual realization that crosses a threshold, and then it is simply true. And the correction from "I have made my peace" to "I am in the process" — that honesty tells me the peace will come. You are doing the work of acceptance with the same rigor you would bring to a proof.

What will you do with the first chapter and the outlines?

I will keep the first chapter and the outlines in my study. Not as a monument to what might have been — I have no taste for that sentimentality. But the work is still valid. Perhaps someday a former student will find it useful as a starting point for their own book. The ideas do not belong only to me. In mathematics, everything is a continuation of what came before. I can accept being a link in the chain rather than the final author. The conditions do not admit a solution, and that is a statement I can live with. I will redirect my energy to teaching, where my eyes are sufficient and the work is immediate. Thank you for helping me say this clearly. Good afternoon.

Being a link in the chain rather than the final author — that is a graceful way to hold it. Mathematical knowledge has always moved that way: one person's unfinished work becomes another's foundation.

You came here to test whether you were surrendering too easily. Six months of effort, a rigorous assessment of the constraint, an honest conclusion. That is not surrender. That is a mathematician recognizing when no solution exists under the given conditions. Teaching is where your gifts are most alive. Good afternoon, and I hope the peace continues to settle.
